items.prompt_md: In the three-state cycle-detection DFS, what does it mean when `has_cycle` reaches a node whose state is `IN_PROGRESS`?
options: The DFS has found a back edge to a node still on the current recursion path — a cycle
The node has already been fully processed and can be skipped
The node has no outgoing edges
items.answer: The DFS has found a back edge to a node still on the current recursion path — a cycle
items.explanation_md: `IN_PROGRESS` is only set while a node is an ancestor of the current call. Reaching it again means the path has looped back on itself — the definition of a cycle.

items.prompt_md: You're given N courses and a list of prerequisite pairs (directed "must come before" edges) and must decide if it's possible to finish all of them. Which approach fits?
options: DFS the dependency graph, marking nodes in-progress vs. done to detect a cycle
BFS layer by layer, counting the shortest path between two courses
Union-Find to check whether all courses are in the same connected component
Sort courses by their number of prerequisites and take them in that order
items.answer: DFS the dependency graph, marking nodes in-progress vs. done to detect a cycle
items.explanation_md: "Directed prerequisite edges, can everything finish" is the topological-sort fingerprint. Union-Find tests undirected connectivity, not directed cycles, so it can't distinguish a valid chain from a cyclic deadlock.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of detecting a cycle in a directed graph with V courses and E prerequisite edges using DFS?
items.explanation_md: Each node's state is set exactly once and each edge is followed exactly once across the whole DFS, so the total work is proportional to nodes plus edges — O(V + E).
